vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
to add more detail or explanation to something you said
b
to express your ideas clearly and effectively in words
c
the list of topics to be discussed in a meeting
d
to give the main points of something, without all the details
e
to review and discuss something after it happens, to learn from it
f
to make something clearer or easier to understand
g
a specific task someone must do after a meeting
h
to take further action after a meeting or message
i
the main point or lesson you keep from a discussion
j
to finish or conclude a meeting or conversation

reading.rtitle: Halfway There: What Real Fluency Sounds Like
paras: Reaching the middle of an intensive program is a good moment to stop and reflect. Fluency is not about speaking fast or never making a mistake. It is about communicating your ideas clearly, even under pressure: being able to articulate a complex plan, clarify a point when someone looks confused, and outline your vision in a way people remember. The professionals who sound most confident are rarely the ones with a perfect accent; they are the ones who organize their thoughts and keep the conversation moving.

One secret of fluent speakers is that they use small phrases to buy time and guide the listener. Instead of freezing when they need a second to think, they say things like 'To give you a bit of context...' or 'What I mean is...'. These discourse markers do two jobs at once: they give the speaker thinking time, and they signal to the listener how the ideas connect. When speakers want to soften a disagreement, they add 'That said...' or 'Having said that...' before making their point.

Reflection also means being honest about what still needs work. After an important call, strong communicators debrief: they ask what went well, what was only developing, and what is now a priority. They write down clear action items, agree on who will follow up, and wrap up with a short summary. The main takeaway is simple: fluency grows fastest when you practice under real pressure and then review honestly what happened.
reading.source: Adapted from a professional-communication guide, The Executive Voice

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?
choices: Fluency means speaking as fast as possible and never making mistakes.
Fluency is about communicating ideas clearly under pressure, using discourse markers and honest review.
The most fluent speakers are always the ones with a perfect accent.

items: Fluency is mainly about having a perfect accent.
The text says confident speakers are rarely the ones with a perfect accent; they organize their thoughts.
Discourse markers like 'What I mean is...' give the speaker thinking time and guide the listener.
The text says these markers do two jobs: they give thinking time and signal how the ideas connect.
'That said...' is used to soften a disagreement before making a point.
The text lists 'That said...' and 'Having said that...' as ways to soften a disagreement.
A debrief only looks at what went well, never at problems.
Strong communicators ask what went well, what was only developing, and what is now a priority.

parts: , I run a fertility clinic in São Paulo and we are about to open a new facility.
, I am looking for a long-term technology partner, not just a supplier.
, the equipment has to be reliable and the support has to be fast.
, I know good technology is expensive.
, for us, quality matters more than price.
bank: To give you a bit of context
The reason I bring this up is

gapfill.text: Notice: discourse markers like 'To give you a bit of context' and 'What I mean is' are not grammar rules -- they are fluency tools. They give you a moment to think and help your listener follow your ideas.

bank.label: Fluency markers and review phrases
items: To give you a bit of context...
The reason I bring this up is...

lf.title: Which marker, and what does it do?
items: To give you a bit of context
, we open the new facility in March.
, I need reliable support, not just cheap machines.
The reason I bring this up is
that I want a real partnership.

lf.text: Notice: 'To give you a bit of context' opens a topic, 'What I mean is' clarifies, 'The reason I bring this up is' explains why it matters, and 'That said' softens a contrast. Use them to sound organized and to buy yourself thinking time.

items.situation: On a video call, you need a second to think before answering a hard question. Start your answer with a phrase that buys time.
tips: To give you a bit of context...

items.situation: The partner looks confused after you explain your clinic's plan. Make your point clearer.
You want to disagree politely about the price, and then still make your point.
The call is ending. Summarize the next steps and say who will do what.
tips: To wrap up, the action items are...

items: Compare where your English was in Lesson 1 with where it is now. Name one area that is strong, one that is still developing, and one priority for the second half of the program.
In under two minutes, introduce yourself and your clinic project to a technology partner. Use at least three discourse markers to stay fluent under pressure.
The partner says your timeline is too ambitious. Respond politely: soften with 'That said...', then defend your plan.
Close the call. Summarize the main takeaway, agree on the action items, and say who will follow up.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: Buy thinking time: 'To give you a bit of context...' / 'What I mean is...'
Explain why it matters: 'The reason I bring this up is that...'
Soften a disagreement: 'That said...' / 'Having said that...'
Debrief honestly: 'What went well was...', 'What is still developing is...', 'My priority now is...'
Wrap up a call: 'To wrap up, the action items are... and I'll follow up by email. The main takeaway is...'
answerkey.note: These are fluency tools, not grammar rules. Use them to organize your ideas and keep the conversation moving under pressure.

listenings.text: Hi Eliane. You are halfway through the program now, so let us take a moment to reflect. When we started, you told me your biggest fear was freezing on an important call. Listen to how far you have come. You can now articulate a complex plan, clarify a point when someone looks confused, and outline your vision in a clear order. What is still developing is your speed when you feel nervous, and that is completely normal. My advice for the second half is simple: keep using your fluency markers, debrief after every real conversation, and be patient with yourself. You are doing beautifully.

Hi Eliane, this is Greg from the medical-technology team in Austin, Texas. Thanks for a great video call earlier. I wanted to follow up and confirm the action items on our side. To give you a bit of context, we work with several new clinics across Latin America, so your project is a perfect fit. We are going to send you a full proposal by Friday, including timelines and support options. That said, if anything is unclear, please call me and I will clarify right away. We are really excited about a long-term partnership with your clinic. Talk soon, and have a great week.
